coredata.dc:title: A sensitive and selective fluorescent sensor for 2,4,6-trinitrophenol detection based on the composite material of magnetic covalent organic frameworks, molecularly imprinted polymers and carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Min
Gao, Manjie
Deng, Linlin
Kang, Xun
Zhang, Kailian
Fu, Qifeng
Xia, Zhining
Gao, Die
coredata.dc:description: An efficient and rapid fluorescent sensor was developed for the sensitive and selective detection of an environmental pollutant, 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP). The fluorescent sensor based composite material was composed of magnetic covalent organic frameworks (MCOFs), molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) and carbon dots (CDs). MCOFs, MIPs and CDs were applied as supporting materials and sorbents, selective sorbents and fluorescent sensor element, respectively. Further, the composite material (MCOFs@MIPs@CDs) was characterized by SEM, TEM, EDS, FT-IR, Zeta potentiometric analysis, XRD, TGA, VSM and the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms. The MCOFs@MIPs@CDs demonstrate good fluorescence properties and stability. When applied for the sensitive detection of TNP, the composite material exhibits good sensitivity for TNP with a LOD of 100 pM, a high imprinting factor of 8.4 (in comparison with MCOFs@NIPs@CDs) and shorter detection time (within 1 min). Moreover, the results of selective experiment further proof that MCOFs@MIPs@CDs have high selectivity to TNP. Finally, the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs based fluorescent method was used to detect TNP in real water samples, and the recoveries (range from 88.7% to 103.4%) demonstrate that the method has the great potential application in the environmental monitoring and evaluation fields.

head.ce:title: A sensitive and selective fluorescent sensor for 2,4,6-trinitrophenol detection based on the composite material of magnetic covalent organic frameworks, molecularly imprinted polymers and carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, Sichuan 646000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, Chongqing 401331, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, Chongqing 401331, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An efficient and rapid fluorescent sensor was developed for the sensitive and selective detection of an environmental pollutant, 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP). The fluorescent sensor based composite material was composed of magnetic covalent organic frameworks (MCOFs), molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) and carbon dots (CDs). MCOFs, MIPs and CDs were applied as supporting materials and sorbents, selective sorbents and fluorescent sensor element, respectively. Further, the composite material (MCOFs@MIPs@CDs) was characterized by SEM, TEM, EDS, FT-IR, Zeta potentiometric analysis, XRD, TGA, VSM and the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms. The MCOFs@MIPs@CDs demonstrate good fluorescence properties and stability. When applied for the sensitive detection of TNP, the composite material exhibits good sensitivity for TNP with a LOD of 100 pM, a high imprinting factor of 8.4 (in comparison with MCOFs@NIPs@CDs) and shorter detection time (within 1 min). Moreover, the results of selective experiment further proof that MCOFs@MIPs@CDs have high selectivity to TNP. Finally, the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs based fluorescent method was used to detect TNP in real water samples, and the recoveries (range from 88.7% to 103.4%) demonstrate that the method has the great potential application in the environmental monitoring and evaluation fields.

ce:def-description.ce:para: poly dimethyl diallyl ammonium chloride solution

ce:section.ce:para: Covalent organic frameworks (COFs), one kind of newly emerging porous crystalline polymers comprising periodically extended and covalently bound network structures, are formed by building blocks covalently linked [1]. Since first discovered in 2005 [2], boronic-acid-based coupling, schiff-based reaction, trimerization of nitriles, carbon-carbon bond formation and etc. become the main reaction types to the formation of COFs [3]. Attributed to the highly ordered porous polymeric network and stable covalent bonding, COFs have shown outstanding characteristics including high chemical and thermal stabilities, large surface areas, low density, orderly arranged pores, uniform pore size and unique delocalized π-π electron system [4]. These characteristics make COFs advantageous in the application in a variety of areas such as extraction [5], sensing [6] and drug delivery [7]. It is worth mentioning that as COFs can supply different forces such as π-π force, hydrogen bonding force, size effect to the substances with particular structures, the applications of COFs as sorbents in the field of extraction such as in waste substances extraction [8], harmful substances and peptides extraction [9] have gained considerable attention. Moreover, due to the highly separation ability, magnetic COFs as sorbents has been highly concerned [10]. However, there are limited researches on the preparation of magnetic COFs which used in the extraction of target substances till now. Hence, more new magnetic COFs based sorbents need to be developed for the extraction of various substances.

Recently, as the environmental pollution becomes more and more serious, the issues of environmental pollution (e.g. air pollution, water pollution) pose a great threat to human health [9,11]. Hence, the enrichment and detection of harmful compounds such as bisphenol [12], nitro-aromatic compounds [13] in environment are of great significance for the security and health of human [14]. 2,4,6-Trinitrophenol (TNP), which is used as a powerful explosive in landmines, a fungicide in agricultural and medical fields, is one of the environmental pollutants [15]. TNP has already been listed in the forefront of the environmental pollutants as it has the characteristics of high water solubility, poor biodegradability and toxicity [16]. Moreover, TNP can cause severe skin irritation, dizziness etc. [17] that it poses great threats to human health. Under this circumstance, selective and sensitive detection method for TNP needs to be developed. In our previous study, COFs were prepared by using 1,3,5-tris(4-aminophenyl)benzene and 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroterephthalaldehyde as two building blocks to the effective adsorption of environmental pollutants such as 2,4-dinitrotoluene, 2,4-difluoronitrobenzene for the first time [18]. This material has the ability to adsorb the nitro-aromatic compounds (with one benzene ring in structure). However, in the previous study, we had not applied it to the adsorption of TNP. Hence, magnetic COFs which used Fe3O4 nanoparticles as magnetic material, COFs [1,3,5-tris(4-aminophenyl)benzene and 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroterephthalaldehyde as two building blocks] as supporting materials and sorbents will be applied to the adsorption of TNP.

Nevertheless, the problem of selectivity to target substance is not solved by using COFs alone as sorbents. As we all know, the interaction effects between COFs and target substances usually are π-π force, hydrogen bonding force, size effect and etc. [19]. Substances with similar structure can be not easily distinguished by using COFs. To improve the selectivity of COFs, molecular imprinting technology which can be applied to prepare molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) has become an attractive and effective technology. MIPs are prepared by copolymerizing template, functional monomer and cross-linker [20]. Due to the special binding sites left by template, MIPs demonstrate high selectivity to target substance [21]. Hence, MIPs can be applied as promising recognition elements with high affinity and selectivity in the adsorption of target substance in complex samples. The selective adsorption ability to target substance may be improved significantly by using the composite material of COFs (as supporting materials and sorbents) and MIPs (as selective sorbents). Recently, combined with COFs and MIPs, several substances such as cano pyrethroids [22], aflatoxin B1 [23] and tyramine [24] have been effectively adsorbed from complex samples. The selectivity of materials improves significantly. However, the report about the preparation of composite materials which are composed of magnetic COFs and MIPs has not been seen yet. Therefore, new composite materials of magnetic COFs and MIPs need to be developed and these materials may become the sorbents with the properties of high selectivity and high adsorption capacity to target substance.

In addition, it is worth noting that excellent sorbents should be equipped with highly sensitive analytical methods. A number of analytical methods such as liquid chromatography [25], mass spectrometry [26], fluorescent method [27] and etc. have been developed for the detection of analytes after adsorption. Among these methods, fluorescent method has been proven to be one of the most outstanding approaches owing to its advantages in sensitivity, simple sample processing process and portability. CDs are a new type of carbon nanomaterials [28] which have been widely used as fluorescence sensor elements for the detection of different analytes such as environmental pollutants [29]. They have gained tremendous attention due to their outstanding properties involving simple preparation, attractive fluorescence properties, biocompatibility, low toxicity and low cost [30]. Usually, CDs can be directly applied to the detection of target substances. For TNP detection, various CDs have already been prepared. For examples, Xie and et al. have successfully prepared amphiphilic CDs and the CDs can be used for TNP detection with the detection limits (LODs) of 0.23–1.31 μM [31]. Chen and co-workers have applied CDs as a fluorescent probe for the detection of TNP and the LOD was 0.48 μM [32]. Although these fluorescent methods are simple, convenient and satisfactory sensitivity can be obtained, the selectivity and the ability to exclude interferences from other substances are less than satisfactory. Fortunately, the advantage of convenient surface functionalization of CDs can link CDs with other materials such as MIPs [33], the composite material of COFs and MIPs [34]. Therefore, new composite material of magnetic COFs and MIPs can be further combined with CDs to form a fluorescent sensor with the properties of high selectivity, high adsorption capacity and high separation speed to target substance. Moreover, due to the size effect of COFs and the specific selectivity of MIPs, the composite material can effectively exclude interferences from target substance. Furthermore, due the properties of CDs, the composite materials also can be directly applied to the sensitivity analysis of target substance (e.g. TNP) by fluorescence method.

Herein, a fluorescent sensor based on magnetic COFs, MIPs and CDs (MCOFs@MIPs@CDs) was synthesized for the first time by using Fe3O4 nanoparticles as magnetic material, COFs as supporting materials and sorbents, MIPs as selective sorbents and CDs as fluorescent sensor element. Moreover, TNP was selected as the target substance to evaluate the performance of the designed fluorescent sensor. The composite material (MCOFs@MIPs@CDs) was characterized by various characterization methods such as SEM, TEM, EDS and etc. Further, the sensitivity and selective detection performances of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs to TNP were systematic studied. Finally, the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs based fluorescent method was successfully applied to the detection of TNP in real water samples and the recoveries results were satisfactory.

2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroterephthalaldehyde and 1,3,5-Tris(4-aminophenyl)benzene obtained from Zhengzhou alfa Chemical Co., Ltd were used for the COF synthesis. Pyrogallol (>98%) was purchased from Zhengzhou JACS Chem Product Co., Ltd. Anhydrous citric acid was obtained from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP, >98%), 2-nitrophenol (>98%), 3-nitrophenol (>98%), 4-nitrophenol (>99%), phenol (>98%), resorcinol (>98%), quinol (>98%),2,4-dinitroaniline (>98%), 2,4-dinitrophenol (>99%), 2,4-dinitrotoluene (>98%), m-dihydroxybenzene (>98%), catechol (>98%), n-hexane, Triton X-100, poly dimethyl diallyl ammonium chloride solution (PDDA), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl-methyldimethoxysilane (AEAPMS) and (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) were obtained from Adamas Reagent Co,. Ltd. Ammonia solution, acetic acid, dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO), tetrahydrofuran (THF), FeCl3•6H2O, ethylene glycol, anhydrous sodium acetate and trisodium citrate dehydrate (Na3Cit.2H2O) were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. Cyclohexane was obtained from Chengdu Jinshan Chemical Co., Ltd. Deionized water obtained from a TKA ultra water purification system was used in all aqueous solutions in whole experiment process.

The Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized according to the reported method [35], and the detailed preparation process was showed in Electronic Supporting Material. For the preparation of MCOFs, 150 mg of Fe3O4, 40 mL DMSO, 79.6 mg of 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroterephthalaldehyde and 31.9 mg of 1,3,5-tris(4-aminophenyl)benzene were added in a 100 mL round-bottomed flask followed by a 5 min ultrasound to form a uniform dispersion. The mixture was reacted in an oil bath at 100 °C after adding 2 mL acetic acid as the catalyst and the brown precipitates were formed quickly (about 5 min). After incubation for 30 min, the synthetic brown precipitate (MCOFs) was separated from the solution by using an external magnetic field. Then, the precipitate was washed with THF and ethanol for several times. Finally, the collected MCOFs were dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for further use.

The CDs were synthesized via the one-step reaction synthetic method which used in our previous work [36]. The preparation process of CDs was described in Electronic Supporting Material. The MCOF@MIPs@CDs were fabricated through a reported reverse microemulsion method with a little modification [37]. All synthesis operations were carried out at room temperature. Firstly, 2 mL n-hexanol and 2 mL Triton X-100 were dispersed in 8 mL cyclohexane under stirring for 30 min in a 50 mL flask. Then, CDs (2 mL) and 4 mg of MCOFs were added. After a 10 min ultrasound, 400 μL of TEOS, 120 μL of ammonia and 200 μL of PDDA aqueous solution were added into the mixture solution in sequence. After a 2 h mechanical stirring, a pre-polymerization solution consisting of 1 mL n-hexanol, 22.9 mg of TNP and 400 μL of APTES which kept for 2 h were added in the mixture and reached for 10 h under continuous stirring. After that, 10 mL acetone were injected the reaction system to break the microemulsion. The MCOFs@MIPs@CDs were firstly washed with 5 mL ethanol for three times and then a mixture of methanol-acetic acid solution (9:1, v/v) was used to remove the templates until no TNP was detected in the eluent by UV–vis spectra. In the process of each washing, 10 mL of mixture was used, which took about 2 min with the help of an external magnetic field. After washing with the methanol until neutral, the final product was collected and dried at 50 °C under a vacuum overnight. As a control, the MCOF@NIPs@CDs were prepared under the same procedure but without the addition of TNP.

A JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope and a JSM-7600F field emission scanning electron microscope (JEOL) were used to evaluate the morphologies of the MCOFs and MCOFs@MIPs@CDs. EDS was conducted on a HORIBA EX-250 energy spectrum (Horiba). FT-IR spectra (4000–400 cm−1) were obtained using KBr pellets on an IR Affinity-1S FT-IR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). The angular X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were collected on an Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer (PSpectris Pte), while the small angle XRD measurements were got using Ultima IV mode of D/max–Rb diffractometer (Rigaku). The nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms were performed on an ASAP 2020 (Micromeritics). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a Shimadzu thermogravimetric analyzer (Shimadzu) from room temperature to 700 °C at a ramp rate of 26 °C min−1. Vibrating sample magnetometer (Microsense-EZ9) was used to measure the magnetic properties. Zeta potential was recorded on a Malvern Nano-ZS instrument (Malvern Instruments). The UV absorption spectra were performed on a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrometer (Shimadzu).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation the adsorption ability of MCOFs
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to evaluate whether MCOFs can be applied as the sorbents for the extraction of TNP, the adsorption ability of MCOFs was evaluated at first. A series of TNP solutions with concentrations ranging from 20 to 900 μg mL−1 were prepared to determine the relationship between the MCOFs and the concentrations of TNP. 4 mg of MCOFs were equilibrated with 2 mL of individual TNP at different concentrations in a 5 mL centrifugal tube and the mixture solution was shaken at 70 rmp for 2 h at room temperature. Then, the MCOFs were separated from the solution by an external magnet. After filtration through a 0.22 μm micro-porous membrane, the supernatant was determined by HPLC. For the adsorption kinetics experiment, 4 mg of MCOFs were added into 2 mL of individual TNP (500 μg mL−1) and the mixture solution was incubated by shaking at 70 rmp for different times (5, 10, 20, 30, 60, 90 and 120 min) at room temperature. The following processes were the same with the static adsorption experiment and the adsorption capacity at equilibrium (Q, mg•g−1) can be calculated with the following formula:
Q =
(
C 0
−
C S
)
*
V m
In this equation, the Q is the adsorption capacity, the initial concentration (μg•mL−1) of TNP is denoted as the C0, while the supernatant concentration of TNP (μg•mL−1) is denoted as the CS. V is the volume of TNP solution (mL) and m (mg) is the weight of MCOFs.

All HPLC analysis was carried out using an Agilent 1290 Infinity II with a diode-array detector (DAD) (Agilent). A ZORBAX SB-C18 (150 mm × 4.6 μm., 5 μm particle size) analytical column was used to analyze TNP. A mixture of 28% acetonitrile (A) and 72% water (B) were used as the mobile phase. The detection wavelength was 370 nm and the flow rate was set as 0.6 mL min−1. The injection volume was 5 μL and the column temperature was maintained at 30 °C.

All the fluorescence spectra of composite material were performed on a LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer), and the fluorescence intensity was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 370 nm for emission over the wavelength range of 400–600 nm. The working solution was obtained by dispersing the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs or MCOF@NIPs@CDs in deionized water, then 2.7 mL working solution and 300 μL of water or different concentration of TNP solution were injected into the fluorescent colorimetric tube sequentially. After mixing at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity was measured by the fluorescence spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pretreatment and analysis of the real complex samples
ce:section.ce:para: Lake water, rain water, dyeing wastewater and match stick solution were obtained from the local in Luzhou and tap water was obtained from our laboratory. Match head black powder was collected and dissolved in water by sonication to obtain the match stick solution. Before the application, all the water samples were pretreated by centrifuging at 12,000 rpm for 20 min and filtering for three times using 0.45 μm micro-porous membrane to remove the impurities. For the tap water, the residual chlorine in water was eliminated firstly by using the heating method before centrifuging. In order to calculate the recoveries of the TNP in the real samples, different concentrations of TNP standard solutions were spiked into the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs solution directly by using standard addition method. The experiment was repeated for five times under the same experimental condition to obtain the relative standard deviation (RSD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Choice of materials to prepare MCOFs
ce:section.ce:para: COFs exhibit many outstanding characteristics such as high chemical and thermal stabilities, low density and high surface area. These characteristics endow them with a great potential useful as excellent sorbents for some organic molecules [38]. The strong π stacking interaction and hydrophobic effect between compounds with COFs (which have abundant π system) also make COFs appropriate as sorbents [39]. Moreover, the core-shell structured MCOFs possess uniform pore size, large external surface and high enrichment factor [40]. Herein, the MCOFs which prepared by using 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroterephthalaldehyde and 1,3,5-tris(4-aminophenyl)benzene via Schiff base reaction were used as supporting materials and sorbents.

The preparation process of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs is illustrated in Scheme 1. The MCOFs were synthesized by using 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroterephthalaldehyde and 1,3,5-tris(4-aminophenyl)benzene through Schiff base reaction in a short time. The MCOFs have many benzene rings, imine groups that they have strong π stacking interaction and hydrophobic effect with TNP. Moreover, the fluorine atoms in structure of MCOFs can supply non-covalent interaction between C-F with the HO in TNP [41]. The CDs were prepared through the decomposition and pyrolysis of anhydrous citric acid and the surface passivation reaction of the amine groups of AEAPMS [34]. Further, an efficient one-pot reverse microemulsion strategy was used to prepare the composite materials of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs. In the reverse microemulsion system, the MCOF can be used as an ideal supporting material to form a co-nuclear structure with CDs. The composite material of MCOFs and CDs can provide not only magnetic performance, but also fluorescent property [3]. As APTES can provide enough -NH2 surface binding sites to CDs, it was used as functional monomer. Moreover, TEOS was applied as the cross-linker. The modified-CDs reacted with MCOFs via the Schiff base reaction. And the partial amino group can further modify the template molecule (TNP) onto the surface of the silica matrix through hydrogen bonds [24]. After the templates were removed by washing with a mixture of methanol-acetic acid solution (9:1, v/v) for several times, the specific recognition sites were successfully formed on the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs.

To confirm whether the composite material was successfully synthesized, the surface morphologies of MCOFs and MCOFs@MIPs@CDs were firstly studied by SEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A and B, it can be easily found that the MCOFs (Fig. 1A) have rough surface, while the surface of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs (Fig. 1B) are much rougher. These results are corresponded to the formation of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs. The TEM image of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs is further showed in Fig. 1C and D. It can be easily seen that the composite material has spherical core-shell structure with the average diameter of about 200 nm. Moreover, MCOFs and CDs are covered by the thin silica layer. In addition, Fig. S2A-S2B and Table S1-S2 show the patterns of EDS measurement of the MCOFs and MCOFs@MIPs@CDs. The results exhibit that of C, N, O, Fe and F elements are present. From the Fig. S2A, the emergence of the peaks of Fe and F indicate that MCOFs have been synthesized. Moreover, in comparison the elemental contents of MCOFs with the elemental contents of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs, the contents of the peaks of N and O increase while the contents of the peaks of Fe and F decrease during the formation of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs. These results indicate that CDs, MCOFs and MIPs exist in the material.

The FT-IR spectra were used to determine the characteristic functional groups of MCOFs and MCOFs@MIPs@CDs. First of all, the FT-IR of 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroterephthalaldehyde and 1,3,5-Tris(4-aminophenyl)benzene were analyzed. As shown in curve a of Fig. 1E, the obvious absorption peaks at 1704 cm−1 and 1298 cm−1 belong to the C = O band and the C-F stretching exists in 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroterephthalaldehyde. Curve b of Fig. 1E shows the FT-IR spectra of 1,3,5-Tris(4-aminophenyl)benzene. It can be found that the NH stretching (3432, 3336 cm−1) and the CN stretching (1273 cm−1) exist [42]. It is worth pointing out that in addition to the characteristic peaks of Fe3O4 (597 cm−1) and the NH (3384 cm−1), new characteristic peak which corresponds to the C = N stretching of imine in COFs at 1631 cm−1 has been emerged in the FT-IR spectrum of MCOFs (curve c) [43]. These results indicate that the MCOFs have been successfully synthesized. The results of the FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4 (curve a), MCOFs@NIPs@CDs (curve b) and MCOFs@MIPs@CDs after the removal of the template (curve c) are showed in Fig. 1F. Firstly, the peak at 595 cm−1 which corresponds to the characteristic vibration of Fe-O appears in these three curves, demonstrating that Fe3O4 is successfully embedded in MCOFs@MIPs@CDs and MCOFs@NIPs@CDs. In addition, it is clearly observed that the FT-IR spectra of MCOFs@NIPs@CDs and MCOFs@MIPs@CDs (after removal of the template) show similar characteristic peaks. The peaks at 1069 cm−1, 808 cm−1 and 436 cm−1 are derived from the asymmetric Si-O-Si stretching, the Si-O vibrations and Si-O-Si bending vibrations, respectively [44]. The appearances of these characteristic peaks indicate that the molecularly imprinted silica is successfully grafted and the polymers have been successfully synthesized. Moreover, most of the template molecules have been removed.

Further, Zeta potential analysis of Fe3O4, MCOFs and MCOFs@MIPs@CDs were determined and showed in Fig. S2C. The Fe3O4 particles display a negative potential of −35.2 mV, while the MCOFs show a negative potential of −9.7 mV after coating with COFs on Fe3O4. The change of potential is attributed to the introduction of the COFs. However, after the introduction of molecularly imprinted silica, the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs display a positive potential of 50.1 mV, indicating that the introduction of imprinted shell further changes the charge on the surface of MCOFs. The magnetic values of Fe3O4, MCOFs and MCOFs@MIPs@CDs were measured at room temperature using vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM) and the results are described in Fig. S2D. It can be obviously seen that all of the samples curves have similar general shape and are symmetrical about the origin in the magnetization curves. The values of Fe3O4, MCOFs and MCOFs@MIPs@CDs are measured as 61.65, 32.82 and 10.97 emu g−1, respectively. These results confirm that these three samples are superparamagnetic and exhibit good magnetism. Furthermore, due to the formation of the COF shell on the surface of Fe3O4, the magnetic value of the MCOFs displays a decrease compared with that of Fe3O4. Moreover, the lower saturation magnetization of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs than MCOFs is due to the formation of composite material. As shown in the inset of Fig. S2D, despite the decrement of the magnetization, the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs still show good dispersion in the solvent and can be collected within 2 min with the help of the external magnetic field. The result demonstrates that MCOFs@MIPs@CDs exhibit good magnetic property and can be used for the rapid magnetic separation.

The thermal stability of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs was performed by TGA. A two-stage decomposition from room temperature to 700 °C can be obviously seen from Fig. S2E. The first-stage decomposition is found at about 350 °C with a weight-loss of 13.2 wt%. This weight-loss is due to the loss of the residual solvent and the decomposition of carboxyl groups in the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs. As the temperature increases to 700 °C, the second-stage decomposition occurs with a 15% weight-loss. This loss is attributed to the decomposition COF shells. Overall, TGA result illustrates that MCOFs@MIPs@CDs have good thermal stability. Further, the X-ray power diffraction (XRD) was applied to analyze the crystalline structure and phase purity of Fe3O4, MCOFs and MCOFs@MIPs@CDs. As shown in Fig. S2F, six relatively discernible diffraction peaks of Fe3O4 at 2θ = 30.3°, 35.7°, 43.4°, 53.2°, 57.0° and 62.5° which appeared in all samples are matched with the crystal indexes of (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) of the crystalline Fe3O4, indicating that the crystalline phase of Fe3O4 is not destroyed during the preparation of the MCOFs and MCOFs@MIPs@CDs [45]. In addition, it can be found that an obvious peak at the corresponding 2θ around 21° are attributed to the structure of CDs from the curve of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs, demonstrating the existence of CDs in MCOFs@MIPs@CDs [36]. Furthermore, the broad diffraction peak at 2θ of 4.3° (Fig. S3A) is attributed to the low crystallinity of the COF shell, suggesting that the MCOFs have been successfully synthesized.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of MCOFs are displayed in Fig. S3B. It can be easily seen that the MCOFs possess mesoporous structure corresponding to the IUPAC classification [46]. Furthermore, according to the data of N2 adsorption-desorption, the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and pore size of MCOFs are calculated as 137.34 m2 g−1 and 4.72 nm (Fig. S3C), respectively. These results show that the MCOFs have high surface to adsorb target molecule and can be further applied as supporting materials and sorbents to form the composite material of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs.

The adsorption isotherms and adsorption kinetics were performed firstly to evaluate the feasibility of the MCOFs as an ideal supporting material and sorbents of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs in the detection of TNP. The adsorption isotherm curves (Fig. S4A) display that as the concentrations of TNP in the solution increase from 20 to 900 μg mL−1, the adsorption amounts of TNP on MCOFs increase. The adsorption reaches adsorption equilibrium at about 500 μg mL−1. The saturation adsorption capacity for TNP is calculated as 166.85 mg g−1. The kinetic adsorption of TNP was investigated by varying the adsorption time from 5 to 120 min. Fig. S4B shows that the adsorption capacity of TNP gradually increases in the first 30 min. After 30 min, the adsorption kinetics curves are generally flat, indicating that the adsorption equilibrium time is about 30 min. These all results reveal that the MCOFs exhibit promising potential to be the ideal sorbents for the extraction of TNP. Moreover, MCOFs can be further combined with MIPs and CDs to form the composite material which has the properties of high selectivity, high adsorption capacity and high separation speed to target TNP.

The fluorescence properties of the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs were investigated by scanning the whole spectra of the composite material and emission spectra at different excitation (320–410 nm) wavelengths. The range of excitation wavelengths is found by scanning the whole spectra of the composite material. Fig. 2 A and B display that the maximum excitation wavelength is 370 nm while the corresponding maximum emission wavelength is about 470 nm. Therefore, further fluorescence measurements of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs were performed under the excitation wavelength at 370 nm and emission wavelength at 470 nm. Moreover, it can be found from Fig. 2C that the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs (without TNP) show stronger fluorescence signal than MCOFs@MIPs@CDs (with TNP) and the fluorescence intensity of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs (without TNP) is restored to 91.3% of MCOFs@NIPs@CDs. These results indicate that the composite material has good fluorescence properties and TNP is successfully removed from the polymers. Further, the fluorescence stability of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs was also studied by testing the changes of fluorescence`in 240 min and every day for 10 days. Results which are shown in Figs. S5A and S5B indicate the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs exhibit good stability. This fluorescence stabilization phenomenon can be explained that the fluorescence intensity of the embedded CDs is much more stable due to the protection of MCOFs and the MIP shell [34]. Hence, the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs show the sufficient stability and have favorable conditions for further TNP detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence measurement of TNP by MCOFs@MIPs@CDs

ce:section.ce:para: To ensure the accuracy of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs in the detection of TNP, the main factors (including the effects of solvent for MCOFs@MIPs@CDs’ dispersion, concentrations of CDs/COF@MMIPs, pH value and the ionic strength of the solution) were optimized. It can be seen from Fig. 3 A, under the same experimental conditions, the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs in aqueous solution provide highest detection performance to TNP than those in ethanol, methanol, acetonitrile, acetone, dichloromethane and tetrahydrofuran, indicating that the water is the suitable dispersion solvent. Then, the effect of the concentration of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs was also investigated (Fig. 3B). It can be seen that the F0/F (fluorescence response intensity) increases as the concentration increases from 4 μg mL−1 to 12 μg mL−1. Therefore, the concentration of 12 μg mL−1 can be chosen as the optimum concentration of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs. The effect of solution pH on the detection of TNP was investigated in a pH range of 2–12. As the pKa value of TNP is 0.38 at 25 °C. When pH > 0.38, TNP exists mainly as an anion state and has a negative charge due to the protonation/deprotonation process [47,48]. Hence, TNP will not be stable in the extreme low pH. Moreover, due to the dissociation of silica shell on the surface of MCOFs@MIPs@CD, extremes of pH value both high and low will affect the binding efficiency of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs and TNP [49]. Therefore, the pH in the range of 2–12 was selected to investigate the optimum pH value. It can be easily found from Fig. 3C that a slightly increase of pH from 2 to 6 lead to an increase of the fluorescence quenching efficiency and the pH from 6 to 12 appears a slightly decrease. This phenomenon may possibly due to the damage of the structure of the binding site by the ionized silica shell [50] . However, the influence on the detection by the change of pH is relatively small, suggesting that the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs can be directly applied to detect TNP in water. Moreover, considering that the ionic strength may have effect on the fluorescence stability of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs, the ion strength of the solution was investigated by adding different concentrations (0 to 2 M) of NaCl. As shown in Fig. 3D, the F0/F of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs to TNP is almost invariable under ionic strength condition, indicating that NaCl has almost no influence on the detection of TNP and MCOFs@MIPs@CDs can be potentially applied for the sensing analytical applications.

In addition, the response time of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs to TNP was also evaluated in 60 min. As shown in Fig. S6, the adsorption process reaches the balance at less than one minute, indicating that the response time is rapid. Compared with the kinetic adsorption of MCOFs to TNP, MCOFs@MIPs@CDs have a much faster time to detect TNP. This result suggests that by combining MCOFs with CDs and MIPs, the detection time can be significantly improved. Moreover, most of the recognition sites are situated at the surface of the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs, which is good for the mass transfer in the detection of TNP. Therefore, the usage of MCOFs as supporting material and sorbents of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs in the detection of TNP is feasible.

Under the optimum conditions, the fluorescence performance of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs was measured by introducing various concentrations (0–100 μM) of TNP. The relationship between fluorescence intensity and the concentration of TNP was determined. The fluorescence quenching is followed the Stern–Volmer equation:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
S V
[
T N P
]
Where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of TNP, respectively. [TNP] is the concentration of the TNP and KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant.

Fig. 4 A and B show that the fluorescence spectra of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs and MCOFs@NIPs@CDs with TNP at different concentrations change, respectively. Compared with MCOFs@NIPs@CDs, the fluorescence intensity of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs is quenched obviously after adding the same concentration of TNP under the same detection conditions. As shown in Fig. 4C, MCOFs@MIPs@CDs have a good line relationship with low range concentrations of 0.3 nM–100 μM. The LOD is found to be 100 pM based on the generalized three times standard deviation of eleven measurements of the blank signal of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs solution, which is lower than those in the previous reported literature (Table 2). This low LOD indicates that the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs can be applied as the a sensitive fluorescent sensor elements for the detection of TNP. The plot of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs is calculated as F0/F = 1.1087 + 0.0302[c] and the correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.9920. As a control, the regression equation of MCOFs@NIPs@CDs is F0/F = 1.0477 + 0.0036 [c] and the R2 is 0.9930. Moreover, the MCOFs@NIPs@CDs exhibit a LOD of 1 μM, which is much higher than that of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs, indicating that MCOFs@MIPs@CDs have good sensitivity. The imprinted factor (IF) is usually used to evaluate the properties of MIPs materials and their selective adsorption to analytes. IF is calculated by the Eq: IF = Ksv, MIP/ Ksv, NIP, while the Ksv is the linear slopes and represents the Stern-Volmer constant [51]. According to the Stern-Volmer equation, the IF can be obtained by comparing the Ksv of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs with the Ksv of MCOFs@NIPs@CDs. In present study, the IF of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs is caluated as 8.4, demonstrating that MCOFs@MIPs@CDs have good selective recognition ability for the detection of TNP. This selectivity can be attributed to the special imprinted cavities produced by TNP in MCOFs@MIPs@CDs during the synthesis process. However, TNP also has slightly fluorescence quenching effect on MCOFs@NIPs@CDs because of the non-specific adsorption of TNP on the material [52]. From the above, it can be speculated that the low LOD and high IF of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs make MCOFs@MIPs@CDs a sensitive fluorescent sensor element with high selectivity for the detection of TNP.

The competitive compounds (structural analogous, concentration of 100 μM) of TNP (Structures of compounds were shown in Fig. S1) and metal ions (100 μM and 5 mM, including Ni2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Na+, K+, Fe2+ and Fe3+) were selected to explore the selectivity of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs and further evaluated the ability of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs to TNP. As shown in Fig. 5 A, MCOFs@MIPs@CDs exhibit significant fluorescence response to TNP than those of other competitive compounds. This phenomenon is mainly due to the inability of the competitive compounds to enter the cavities on the surface of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs and hence the fluorescence quenching effects can be not induced. Nevertheless, there is no obvious change in fluorescence response among the twelve compounds by using MCOFs@NIPs@CDs and all the analogous have little effect. This result is mainly attributed to the non-specific binding sites on the surface of MCOFs@NIPs@CDs. These all results indicate that MCOFs@MIPs@CDs have better binding capacity for TNP due to the specific cavities left by the removal of the template.

Furthermore, the influences of metal ions on the fluorescence of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs solution were also explored as real samples usually exist metal ions. It can be obtained from Fig. 5B and C that the fluorescence responses of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs in the absence and presence of these metal ions (high or low concentration) are almost unchanged, indicating that metal ions have almost no effects on the fluorescence of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs. Therefore, the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs exhibit high selectivity in the application of TNP detection.

The stability and reusability of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs to TNP are also important factors for evaluating the performance of the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs before the detection of TNP in real samples. The stability of the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs to TNP was investigated by testing the changes of fluorescence response intensity every day for 10 days. It can be easily seen from Fig. S7A that the fluorescence response intensity changes slightly, suggesting that the fluorescence response intensity of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs to TNP is sufficiently stable. This stability is largely attributed to the protection of the MCOFs and the MIP shell. The reusability was studied by using MCOFs@MIPs@CDs for the detection of TNP for 8 cycles. It can be obviously seen from Fig. S7B that the fluorescence response intensity has no significant reduction after 8 cycles, indicating that the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs have good reusability and have the potential to detect TNP in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism study of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs to TNP
ce:section.ce:para:
It is reported that the fluorescence quenching mechanism can be divided into two types, one is static quenching and the other is dynamic quenching. When static quenching takes place, there may be a formation of a binary complex and the lifetime of fluorescence molecule on excited state has no change in the presence of quencher. However, if dynamic quenching occurs, the fluorescence lifetime become shorter [53]. That is mean when the ratio of the τ0 and τ is 1(τ0 is the average lifetime of the molecule and τ is the lifetime of fluorescence molecular in the presence of quencher), the quenching of fluorescence is a static quenching process. For dynamic quenching, τ0/τ = F0/F (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs in the absence and presence of TNP, respectively). Since the measurement of fluorescence lifetime is the most definitive method to distinguish the two quenching process, the τ0 and τ of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs were measured. As shown in Fig. 6
A, the average fluorescence lifetime of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs is about 14.75 ns. After adding TNP into MCOFs@MIPs@CDs, the fluorescence lifetime is 14.58 ns. The little change between 14.75 ns and 14.58 ns demonstrates that there is no significant electron-transfer process between MCOFs@MIPs@CDs and TNP. Hence, the quenching may be a static quenching process. Furthermore, the UV–vis spectra of TNP and the fluorescence spectra of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs were studied to further investigate the quenching mechanism. Fig. 6B shows that there is an obvious overlapped spectrum between the excitation spectra of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs and the absorption band of TNP. This phenomenon indicates that the TNP can efficiently shield the excitation light for MCOFs@MIPs@CDs and this is a remarkable characteristic of static quenching. What's more, the reaction was carried out at different temperatures to obtain the binding constant for the formation of the complex. The fluorescence quenching is followed the Stern-Volmer equation which can be expressed as follows [54]:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
s v
[ c ]
= 1 +
K q
t 0
[ c ]
Ksv is the quenching constant of the Stern-Volmer, Kq is the quenching rate constant and [c] is the TNP quencher concentration. Ksv in different reaction temperature was studied. As shown in Fig. 6C and Table S3, Ksv reduces as the reaction temperature increases. This phenomenon is a remarkable characteristic of static quenching. Moreover, according to Ksv= Kqτ0, the quenching rate constant Kq are calculated as 2.16 L mol−1 s−1(at 25 °C), 1.89 L mol−1 s−1(at 40 °C), and 1.84 L mol−1 s−1(at 60 °C), respectively. All the Kq of this experiment are far exceeded the biggest Kq of collisional quenching (1–2 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1), further demonstrating that the fluorescence quenching of TNP on MCOFs@MIPs@CDs is a static quenching process [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs to real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the realistic application and the feasibility of this method, the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs were used for the determination of TNP in real water samples. Before doing the recovery tests, the fluorescence response of TNP and the influences of selected water samples on fluorescence response and the UV absorption were measured at first. Fig. S8A and S8B show that TNP has no fluorescence response. Moreover, the selected water samples have almost no background UV absorption and no influence on fluorescence of MCOFs@MIPs@CDs solution. Therefore, the water samples can be used for further recovery tests. As listed in Table 1 , the relative recoveries of TNP obtained from the real samples range from 88.7% to 103.4% at three spiking concentrations and the intraday relative standard deviation (RSD) ranges from 0.22 to 5.76%, by repeating the same samples for 5 times. Table 2 lists the other reports used for the detection of TNP (including LOD and linear range). In comparison with those works, the method in this study exhibits high sensitivity and specificity with lower LOD. From these results, it can be summarized that this method which used MCOFs@MIPs@CDs as fluorescence detection sensor element can be applied for the sensitive and selective detection of TNP in real samples.

In summary, a sensitive and selective fluorescent sensor which based on the composite material of MCOFs, MIPs and CDs (MCOFs@MIPs@CDs) was successfully developed for the first time. In the preparation of the MCOFs@MIPs@CDs, MCOFs were applied as supporting material and sorbents, MIPs were used as selective sorbents and the CDs were selected as the fluorescent sensor element. The MCOFs@MIPs@CDs were synthesized successfully using a one-pot reverse microemulsion polymerization method and were applied for the sensitive, selective and rapid detection of TNP. The MCOFs@MIPs@CDs exhibit obvious characteristics including the magnetism, good reusability, long-term stability, sensitivity (LOD of 100 pM), high selectivity (IF=8.4) and shorter detection time (within 1 min) for TNP. Furthermore, the novel fluorescent sensor were successfully used to the detection of TNP in real water samples, and the recoveries of TNP demonstrate that MCOFs@MIPs@CDs can be applied to rapidly and accurately detect and monitor TNP in water samples. This fluorescent sensor exhibits the great potential application in the environmental monitoring and evaluation fields.
